titleStmt.title: A Transcription of John in Codex Usserianus primus
VL14
The New Testament
The Gospel according to John
john
titleStmt.funder: The Arts and Humanities Research Council

sponsor.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing

publisher.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing
University of Birmingham

availability.p: Licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0): available for re-use provided attribution is made to the original creators (the IGNTP and any named participants) and an indication is given of any changes made. See further .

msItem.title: Quattuor Euangelia
Four Gospels in order Mt Jo Lc Mc

condition.p: Damage to all four margins; text readable to Abbott in 1884 is not always legible now.

decoDesc.p: Black ink; chapter headings in a coloured ink (green? red?)

surrogates.bibl: Black and white photographs in ITSEE, supplied by Vetus Latina-Institut.

listBibl.bibl: High-resolution colour digital images on CD in ITSEE supplied by TCD.

projectDesc.p: This transcription was made by the Verbum Project as part of an online and printed edition of the Old Latin versions of John between 2003 and 2010.

editorialDecl.p: This transcription covers ff. 34r-78r. Lacunose in 1:1-15. Chapter numbers occur in the margin (Type I), with the first line of each new chapter in a coloured ink, which has now largely faded.The transcription includes foliation, lineation, all punctuation (including the size of spaces) and abbreviations. Text visible to Abbott but not discernible on our images is tagged as such. The initial transcription file was a plain text file for use with COLLATE. Further information about the procedures followed may be found in the electronic edition at
This second edition of the file was converted to XML by Catherine Smith using the project's Python conversion scripts 1.0.
The XML specifications used are version 1.5 of the IGNTP Guidelines available at

revisionDesc.change: Adjustment of transcription to version 1.5 of the XML by Hugh Houghton.
Reconversion of the original plain text file using Python scripts to version 1.4 of the XML by Catherine Smith and manual checking.
Hyphen entity replaced by lb attribute break="no" by Hugh Houghton
Changes made in order to display properly with VLI.xsl transformation by Hugh Houghton
Correction to John 4:7 and 4:16, and change to verse division in 5:18-19 by Hugh Houghton
Transcription converted to the TEI XML schema and corrected for parsing by Hugh Houghton

ab.note: Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott marks punctuation here, but it is not in the usual place and seems to be a spot on the ms
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Pattern at end of line; two commas and eighteen cola.
Abbott records that this line is written in display script (rubricated)
Abbott marks 'uidit eos sequisse' as written in display script (rubricated)
Abbott reads ei, but this is likely to be part of eum
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott marks the word as unreadable
This word is omitted by Abbott
There appears to be a dot above the 'o' and a line above the 'u' which many be a correction of 'de nouo' to 'denuo'.
Hole in original parchment in lines 19-20.
The last word is probably 'omnis '; compare Abbott 's 'n ' for 'm ' in John 2:4
Hole in original parchment in lines 19-21.

There is little variation in the other Latin versions at this point, but there does not seem to be enough space in Codex Usserianus for the text found in other manuscripts. It is possible that folio 37v or 38r had 23 rather than the usual 22 lines of text, or that one line was omitted.
Julicher and Abbott read ini(ectus), but 'mi' is clear in the manuscript.

According to Abbott, from *pater* to *credit* in "display script" (rubrics); but black ink clearly picks up at 'qui credit'. Also Abbott says that there is a numeral *iiii* in the margin before line 14, which cannot be discerned from the images available.
Abbott marks the display script (rubrication) as ending after ih&nasalbar;s; it is impossible to tell from the images.
Abbott alterutorum but corrected in corrigenda
Abbott does not mark the correction (by overdotting)
Unlike the correction earlier on this page, the letter is struck out by a thin line, indicating a later corrector.
This line is omitted by Abbott
On line 6, Abbott reads 'tibentibus', with the five middle letters 'benti' overdotted. The overdots can be seen in the manuscript, but not what preceded them.
Abbott does not note the first-hand reading
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott records punctuation here, but it is abnormal and more likely to be damage
Abbott notes in a footnote that 'ante i partim exstant xi', although this projects into the margin as if it were a chapter number.
The line-numbering of F 48r given here does not follow Abbott, who regards this as a 23-line page; there are no textual grounds for assuming that the top line was preceded by one no longer visible.
The remaining portion of the penultimate letter of this line appears more likely to be 'u', despite 'a' in Jülicher.
The line-numbering of F 49r (and F 49v) given here does not follow Abbott, who regards these as 21-line pages; we see no grounds for this.
The line-numbering of F 49v (and F 49r) given here does not follow Abbott, who regards these as 21-line pages; we see no grounds for this.
Abbott does not record any text visible on this line.
Abbott does not record any text visible on this line
Abbott does not record the digraph
Abbott does not record the punctuation
There is a space of three letters, which may be erased rubric, or part of the decorative pattern.
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott records the original reading as abrahe, corrected to abrahae.
Abbott corrects 'in ' to 'iu ' in his corrigenda
Abbott reads 'ex.teram' and notes 'x supra u script.', but there is no sign of this on our images.
Abbott records that this whole line is written in display script (rubricated), although only part is visible on the images.
Abbott intrabit - the letter is only partially visible on our images, but the pen strokes suggest 'u '
Abbott does not record the punctuation at the end of this verse, which might even be part of a letter
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott does not record the punctuation
possible letters in red, now illegible
Abbott reads 'haec ' for 'haec dicit '; the manuscript is damaged at this point, but the remaining ink marks fit 'haec dicit '
Abbott does not record the punctuation
The correction is not noted by Abbott

Abbott records a correction from 'accesserant' to 'ascenderant'. However, the 'd' appears to be original, as it has been cancelled with a diagonal stroke, and then restored (as in the line below). It therefore seems likely that there were three stages of correction by the first hand: accessderant > accesserant > ascenderant.
Abbott only sees this as a one-stage correction
Abbott gives the initial reading as 'ascenderant '
The amount of space would suggest an additional word here.
Abbott omits the whole of this line
Abbott claims that ]orte has been corrected from ]urte, but does not record the correction of the subsequent word.
Abbott does not record the correction
Abbott does not record the punctuation
The space suggests that there may have been a placeholder, or a word no longer visible.
The space is too small for 'esaiae' in full unless it was written in the margin. The line above is too dark and misplaced for a nomen sacrum.
Abbott does not record the nasal bar
Abbott does not record the correction
Abbott odes not record the punctuation

Abbott suggests that the final 's' of 'cognoscetis' has been added by a corrector. Although the space is narrow, the ink and letter shape is very similar to the first hand; it is possible that both 'i' and 's' were a first-hand correction.
Abbott does not record the correction
Abbott does not record the correction
te can be seen added super lineam
Abbott says that the nasal bar has been added by a later hand, but it is similar to the overline on spiritus later in the page, so may be by the first-hand.
Abbott only records five dots; nine are visible in our images
The rubricated text is actually a green colour and better preserved than earlier examples.
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott does not record the punctuation
Abbott records the whole word 'interroget' as cancelled by expunction.
Abbott records six dots; ten are visible

div.ab:
[rub]xi[/rub]Abbott gives the chapter number as 'xi ', which fits the sequence, but in the images it looks more like 'ix 'haeclocutusestihsetlaeuauitoculos
suosincaelumetdixitpateruenithoraho
norificafiliumtuumutfiliustuushonori
ficette

ab.note: Abbott does not record the correction
The words 'gloriam meam quam mihi' (and possibly 'dedisti') are in a slightly smaller script.
According to Abbott, the &nasalbar; of 'constitutione&nasalbar;' was added by a second hand.
Abbott records punctuation after huius, not visible on photos
Abbott does not include any of this word
Abbott does not include the superline
Abbott does not see this word
The ink mark at the beginning of the line is not consonant with 'm '
Abbott reads meo but does not note the grammar as he does elsewhere
